name: Castillo de Cornago and ruins of Turruncún – circular from Cornago
name_original: Castillo de Cornago y ruinas de Turruncún – circular desde Cornago
region_intro: Touring cycling around Cornago offers routes through the Sierra de Alcarama mountains and the Linares river valley. The region features varied terrain, from gently rolling sections to challenging ascents, characterized by sedimentary rocks including limestones, marls, and sandstones. Cyclists can navigate quiet country lanes and paths that traverse a landscape of hills and mountainous areas. This diverse environment provides a range of experiences for touring cyclists. Best touring cycling routes around Cornago The most popular touring cycling route is Cervera del Río Alhama – Church of Castilruiz loop from Cervera del Río Alhama, a 47.0 miles (75.7 km) trail that takes 5 hours 26 minutes to complete. This difficult route explores the mountainous terrain and historical sites of the region. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Cidacos Greenway – Autol and its Picuezos loop from Villarroya, a difficult 37.5 miles (60.3 km) path. This route follows a greenway, offering varied terrain through the landscape. Local touring cyclists also love the Castillo de Cornago and ruins of Turruncún – circular from Cornago, a 25.1 miles (40.4 km) trail leading through the Sierra de Alcarama mountains and past historical ruins, often completed in about 3 hours 19 minutes. Touring cycling around Cornago is defined by the Sierra de Alcarama mountains, the Linares river valley, and quiet country lanes. The network offers options for challenging ascents and varied terrain. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 30 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Cornago's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Cornago?
A: There are 6 touring cycling routes around Cornago listed on komoot. These routes offer a variety of experiences through the Sierra de Alcarama mountains and the Linares river valley.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes near Cornago?
A: The terrain around Cornago is quite varied, ranging from gently rolling hills to more challenging ascents, particularly in the Sierra de Alcarama mountains. You'll encounter routes with significant elevation changes, and some may include unpaved sections. The geological makeup, with sedimentary rocks like limestones and sandstones, contributes to this diverse landscape.

Q: Are there any family-friendly touring cycling routes in Cornago?
A: The touring cycling routes listed for Cornago are generally rated as difficult, suggesting they might be more suitable for experienced cyclists rather than families with young children. The region's varied terrain and significant elevation changes contribute to this difficulty. For easier options, you might look for dedicated greenways or flatter sections in the broader La Rioja region.

Q: Can I find circular touring cycling routes around Cornago?
A: Yes, there are circular touring cycling routes available. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e994468383/castillo-de-cornago-and-ruins-of-turruncun-circular-from-cornago" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo de Cornago and ruins of Turruncún – circular from Cornago&lt;/a&gt; route is a 40.4 km loop that takes you through the Sierra de Alcarama mountains and past historical ruins.

Q: What historical sites or natural features can I see while cycling near Cornago?
A: Cycling near Cornago offers a rich blend of history and nature. You can visit the impressive 12th-century &lt;a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Castle_of_Cornago" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cornago Castle&lt;/a&gt;, which crowns the hill above the village. Other historical sites include the 16th-century Baroja Palace and the San Pedro Church. For natural features, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4787673" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Views of Cornago from the GR 93&lt;/a&gt; offer stunning panoramas, and the Sierra de Alcarama mountains provide a beautiful backdrop.

Q: Where can I see dinosaur footprints (Icnitas) while touring cycling?
A: Approximately 3 kilometers from Cornago, you can visit the Icnitas archaeological site in Los Cayos, which preserves numerous dinosaur footprints from the Cretaceous period. The broader area around Enciso also features many Icnitas deposits, and a paved road connects Enciso and Cornago, making these sites accessible by bike.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Cornago?
A: While specific recommendations for Cornago are not provided, La Rioja generally offers pleasant cycling conditions during spring and autumn. These seasons typically avoid the intense heat of summer and the colder, potentially wetter conditions of winter, making for more comfortable rides through the varied terrain.

Q: Are there options for advanced or long-distance touring cyclists?
A: Yes, the routes around Cornago are generally rated as difficult, with significant distances and elevation gains, making them suitable for advanced and long-distance touring cyclists. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31816062" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cervera del Río Alhama – Church of Castilruiz loop from Cervera del Río Alhama&lt;/a&gt; is 75.7 km long with substantial climbing, offering a challenging experience.

Q: What are the parking options for touring cyclists in Cornago?
A: Cornago is a small village, and while specific dedicated cycling parking facilities are not detailed, you can generally find parking within or on the outskirts of the village. It's advisable to look for public parking areas or consult local information upon arrival, especially if you are starting a route directly from Cornago.

Q: Is public transport available to access cycling routes near Cornago?
A: Information on specific public transport options for cyclists to access routes directly from Cornago is limited. La Rioja has made efforts to improve infrastructure for cycle tourists, but for reaching specific trailheads, especially in mountainous areas, personal transport might be more convenient. It's recommended to check local bus schedules or regional transport services for connections to nearby towns if you plan to use public transport.

Q: What makes Cornago a good destination for touring cyclists?
A: Cornago is an excellent destination for touring cyclists due to its diverse landscapes, quiet country roads, and rich historical context. You can enjoy challenging rides through the Sierra de Alcarama mountains, explore the Linares river valley, and integrate visits to medieval castles like &lt;a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Castle_of_Cornago" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cornago Castle&lt;/a&gt; and unique archaeological sites like the Icnitas dinosaur footprints into your cycling adventures.

Q: Are there any routes that offer scenic viewpoints?
A: Many routes in the Sierra de Alcarama mountains offer breathtaking views. The village of Cornago itself is situated on a hillside, providing scenic vistas, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4787673" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Views of Cornago from the GR 93&lt;/a&gt; highlight is specifically noted for its panoramic outlooks. Cycling through this mountainous region will consistently reward you with stunning natural scenery.

Q: What kind of bikes are recommended for touring cycling in this region?
A: Given the varied terrain, which includes both paved roads and potentially unpaved sections, a hybrid bike or a touring bike with robust tires would be well-suited for exploring the Cornago region. These bikes offer a good balance of comfort and durability to handle the mix of surfaces and elevation changes you might encounter.
tips: At the top of the Cornago hill is a beautiful 18th century castle, built during the Late Middle Ages. Rectangular in plan with a tower at each vertex, the castle was built in ashlar masonry on the ruins of an old fortress from the 9th century.

Located at an altitude of 750 meters, within the Sierra de Alcarama, the town of Cornago has a long history, since the first documents found on the population date back to the 9th century.

The inhabitants of Cornago mainly worked in the agricultural sector until the arrival of the textile industry during the 19th century. With the closure of this industry and the decline of agriculture, during the 60s of the 20th century the town caused a great demographic decline.

The town stands out for its 18th century castle, raised above the houses, with privileged views of the town and the valley.

Turruncún is an abandoned town located in the Sierra de Préjano, in the Rioja Baja region.

The coal mines were the great economic engine of the town during the 20th century, when the municipality reached about 300 inhabitants. When the mines were closed, the town slowly decreased until it was completely abandoned.

The ruined village was annexed to the municipality of Arnedo in 1975. Today you can still see the church and an old school, as well as the walls of some of the houses.
